candidates.params_size: ~0.4B (F32 checkpoint ~1.4 GB; fp16 runtime ~1 GB + vocoder) — fits easily in 8 GB
candidates.install: pip install git+https://github.com/AI4Bharat/IndicF5.git, then transformers AutoModel.from_pretrained('ai4bharat/IndicF5', trust_remote_code=True)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Fits in memory, but F5-family flow-matching is compute-heavy: MPS support is partial with CPU fallback (GitHub issue #875), so expect RTF well above 1 on an M2 8GB via stock PyTorch; f5-tts-mlx reaches RTF ~0.15 only on M3 Max/M4 Pro class chips and would need weight conversion for IndicF5. Usable for offline benchmarking on M2, not live latency; runs fine on Colab T4.
candidates.quality_evidence: AI4Bharat's flagship 'near-human' polyglot model, trained on 1,417 h (Rasa, IndicTTS, LIMMITS, IndicVoices-R); widely treated as the best open Hindi voice-cloning quality. Known Hinglish weakness: raw Latin-script/English spans are silently dropped or garbled because ASCII embeddings are undertrained — production users transliterate English words to Devanagari ('WhatsApp'→'व्हाट्सऐप') first (arXiv 2604.25441 analysis). Numbers safest when written out in native script. Live demo Space available.

languages: plus 14 more (21 total incl. Maithili, Sanskrit, Bodo)
candidates.params_size: ~0.9B (F32 ~3.6 GB on disk; load fp16 ~1.8 GB) — fits in 8 GB
candidates.install: pip install git+https://github.com/huggingface/parler-tts.git
candidates.mac_feasibility: Loads fine on M2 8GB in fp16, but autoregressive decoder + DAC is slow off-GPU: community reports ~10–15 s to generate a 10–15 word sentence without a strong GPU (HF discussion #10). Fine for offline MOS/WER benchmarking, poor latency-to-first-audio on Mac; comfortable on Colab T4 with SDPA/compile optimizations.
candidates.quality_evidence: Broadest official Indic coverage (21 languages, 69 named voices, description-based control of emotion/pitch/rate). Native-speaker MOS-like score for Hindi: 83.43 pretrained → 84.79 fine-tuned. No true cloning — speaker identity via named preset voices + text description. Handles Devanagari natively; code-mixed Hinglish works via its English support but is not a documented strength.

candidates.params_size: 82M (~330 MB fp32, ~170 MB fp16) — trivial for 8 GB
candidates.install: brew install espeak-ng && pip install kokoro soundfile (KPipeline lang_code='h'); MLX port: mlx-community/Kokoro-82M-bf16 via mlx-audio; ONNX via kokoro-onnx
candidates.mac_feasibility: The one model that is unambiguously comfortable on M2 8GB: faster than real-time on CPU (RTF < 1), with native MLX and ONNX ports for Apple Silicon. Best latency-to-first-audio of any Hindi option here.
candidates.quality_evidence: Community feedback thread 'Hindi Voices are very good' rates them ~level B despite the maintainer confirming only single-digit hours of Hindi training data (HF discussion #88). Hindi G2P goes through espeak-ng fallback (not dedicated Misaki rules): Devanagari works, Misaki normalizes numbers, but Latin-script Hinglish gets anglicized pronunciation. 2F+2M preset voices only, no cloning.

candidates.params_size: ~0.5B Llama backbone + s3gen decoder; several-GB checkpoint download. Community Mac guides state 8 GB RAM minimum, 16 GB recommended for multilingual + cloning
candidates.install: pip install chatterbox-tts (use device='mps' on Mac; community Apple Silicon patches exist)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Borderline but real: MPS is supported via community-adapted code (Jimmi42 Apple Silicon port), yet 8 GB is the stated floor and a memory leak on Apple Silicon was reported (GitHub issue #218) — expect short-clip offline use only on M2 8GB, and comfortable operation on Colab T4.
candidates.quality_evidence: Zero-shot cloning from ~10 s of reference audio in all 23 languages; Resemble's docs explicitly list Hindi among the 7 most stable languages. First open model with emotion-exaggeration control; all output carries the PerTh neural watermark (relevant for benchmarking round-trip WER — watermark survives compression). One of the strongest MIT-licensed Hindi cloning options.

candidates.params_size: 3B Llama-based + SNAC 24 kHz codec (~6 GB fp16; ~2 GB with NF4 4-bit)
candidates.install: transformers + snac + bitsandbytes (NF4 4-bit); no official Mac path (bitsandbytes is CUDA-only)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Not realistic on M2 8GB: 3B fp16 (~6 GB) plus SNAC decode plus OS would swap, and the documented 4-bit path (NF4 + bitsandbytes) requires CUDA. Third-party conversions exist only for Rockchip NPUs, not MLX. Treat as a Colab T4 candidate (fits in 4-bit).
candidates.quality_evidence: Purpose-built Indian TTS: self-reported MOS 4.2/5.0, RTF 0.05 and <80 ms latency on H100; 4 preset Indian voices (kavya, agastya, maitri, vinaya); explicitly designed for Hindi-English code-mixed input. No zero-shot cloning. Independent review (qcall.ai) flags real-world performance issues; Tamil/Telugu/Bengali planned but not shipped.

candidates.params_size: Llama-3.2-3B base (~4B with audio-token heads); AWQ W4A16 and INT8 quants published by devnagriai
candidates.install: vLLM or transformers + snac; quantized: devnagriai/snorTTS-Indic-v0-AWQ-W4A16
candidates.mac_feasibility: Not practical on M2 8GB (3–4B LLM-TTS, CUDA/vLLM-oriented tooling, no MLX port). Fits a Colab T4 using the published AWQ 4-bit quant. Built on canopylabs/3b-hi-pretrain (Orpheus Hindi research release), which itself has GGUF Q8 conversions if you want a llama.cpp experiment.
candidates.quality_evidence: The most interesting late-2025 Indic release for code-switching: demos show fluent mixing across Hindi/Tamil/Bengali and English within one utterance, plus voice cloning and cross-lingual voice transfer across 9 Indic languages. Trained for ~$6 of compute on ~135 h; full code+data open (blog walkthrough). Young project — quality is promising per demos but has no formal MOS evals yet.

candidates.license: CPML 1.0 (non-commercial only; Coqui defunct since Jan 2024, no commercial license purchasable)
candidates.params_size: ~750M (~1.9 GB download) — fits in 8 GB
candidates.install: pip install coqui-tts (maintained idiap fork of coqui-ai/TTS)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Runs on M2 8GB on CPU at roughly RTF 1–3 (MPS support partial); memory fits comfortably. Streaming inference (<200 ms first chunk) only realistic on CUDA — treat Mac use as offline.
candidates.quality_evidence: 6-second-sample cloning with cross-lingual transfer covering both Hindi and Arabic in one model — useful as the single 'all three case-study languages' baseline. Hindi output is intelligible but noticeably accented/less natural than IndicF5; community Hindi fine-tunes exist (e.g. Abhinay45/XTTS-Hindi-finetuned). Aging architecture; CPML blocks commercial use.

languages: Hindi (sibling checkpoints for Tamil/Telugu/Bengali and 1,100+ languages)
candidates.params_size: ~36M VITS (~150 MB) — negligible
candidates.install: transformers >= 4.33: VitsModel.from_pretrained('facebook/mms-tts-hin')
candidates.mac_feasibility: Trivial on M2 8GB — single-pass VITS, faster than real-time on CPU with tiny memory. The safest 'always works' floor for the benchmark table.
candidates.quality_evidence: Single fixed voice, 2023-era VITS naturalness — clearly below IndicF5/Parler in MOS but perfectly intelligible; useful as latency/WER baseline rather than a 'sounds human' contender. No cloning, no expressiveness control; Devanagari input via built-in uroman tokenizer, no Hinglish handling.

summary: For Hindi on an M2 8GB, the practical shortlist is a two-tier story: Kokoro-82M (Apache-2.0, 4 Hindi voices, real-time on CPU/MLX but no cloning) is the only fully comfortable local model, while IndicF5 (MIT, 11 Indic languages, best open Hindi cloning quality but slow off-CUDA and it garbles raw Latin-script Hinglish — transliterate English words to Devanagari first) and Indic Parler-TTS (Apache-2.0, 21 Indic languages incl. Tamil/Telugu/Bengali, Hindi MOS-like ~84.8, no cloning, ~10-15 s/sentence off-GPU) fit in memory but are offline-benchmark-only on the Mac. Chatterbox Multilingual (MIT, Sept 2025) is the strongest commercially-clean cloning option that lists Hindi among its most stable languages and also covers Arabic and English, though 8 GB is its stated floor on Apple Silicon. The 3B LLM-TTS wave — Veena (Hindi/Hinglish presets, MOS 4.2 self-reported) and snorTTS-Indic-v0 (9-language cloning + code-switching, built on Orpheus's Hindi research release) — is Apache-licensed and impressive but realistically Colab-T4-only via 4-bit quants. XTTS-v2 (CPML non-commercial) remains the one model covering Hindi+Arabic+English with 6-second cloning on-device, and MMS-TTS-hin (CC-BY-NC) is a tiny fixed-voice baseline; AI4Bharat's legacy Indic-TTS FastPitch repo (MIT, 13 languages, https://github.com/AI4Bharat/Indic-TTS) is CPU-friendly but 2023-era with no cloning. Sarvam AI's Bulbul V3 (Feb 2026) stays API-only with no open TTS weights, so it is disqualified for the core pipeline.

candidates.model: Chatterbox family (Chatterbox 0.5B / Chatterbox-Turbo 350M / Chatterbox-Multilingual V3 0.5B) — Resemble AI
candidates.license: MIT (all variants; outputs carry PerTh watermark by default)
languages: 23+ languages incl. Arabic and Hindi (Multilingual V3)
candidates.params_size: 350M (Turbo) / 500M (base & multilingual); MLX fp16 checkpoint ~1.3 GB; comfortably under 4 GB total runtime
candidates.mac_feasibility: Strong. Community Apple Silicon ports exist: mlx-audio supports Chatterbox, Jimmi42/chatterbox-turbo-apple-silicon (MLX, emotion tags + cloning), Jimmi42/chatterbox-tts-apple-silicon (MPS-fixed app). Multilingual MLX fp16 is ~1.3 GB, so it fits 8 GB unified memory with headroom; expect roughly real-time (RTF ~1) for the 0.5B base on a base M2 and faster for Turbo (RTF 0.499 with 472 ms first-chunk latency reported on RTX 4090 via the streaming fork; Turbo claims 75 ms latency / 6x real-time on GPU). This is the primary cloning-capable candidate that fits 8 GB.
candidates.install: pip install chatterbox-tts (CUDA/MPS PyTorch); Mac: pip install mlx-audio (Chatterbox supported) or Jimmi42 Apple Silicon repos; streaming: davidbrowne17/chatterbox-streaming fork adds generate_stream()
candidates.quality_evidence: Highest-profile open-weight model of 2025-26: reported 63.75% blind-test preference vs ElevenLabs; #1 open-weight on Artificial Analysis arena in early-2026 snapshots (ELO ~1123 per OfflineTTS roundup; 1011 in the July 2026 provider-voice snapshot). Turbo evaluated on Podonos vs ElevenLabs Turbo/Cartesia Sonic 3. Native paralinguistic tags ([laugh],[cough]) in Turbo.
candidates.release_date: Chatterbox May 2025; Multilingual Sep 2025; Turbo late 2025

candidates.model: Qwen3-TTS (12Hz-0.6B-Base / 12Hz-1.7B-Base, + CustomVoice / VoiceDesign variants) — Alibaba Qwen
candidates.params_size: 0.6B and 1.7B; MLX-quantized 0.6B runs in ~2-3 GB RAM (community-reported) vs 10+ GB for PyTorch
candidates.mac_feasibility: Strong for 0.6B (and workable for 1.7B 4/8-bit). MLX-Audio gained Qwen3-TTS support within weeks of release; dedicated Mac repos (kapi2800/qwen3-tts-apple-silicon, suckerfish/qwen3-tts-mlx, hiroki-abe-58/Qwen3-TTS-Mac-GeneLab with 8/4-bit quant) report 2-3 GB RAM usage and working 5-second voice cloning fully offline on M1-M4. Official stack quotes 97 ms end-to-end streaming latency on GPU; expect comfortably faster than real time for 0.6B MLX on M2.
candidates.install: Mac: pip install mlx-audio (or kapi2800/qwen3-tts-apple-silicon); GPU: official repo pip install with FlashAttention 2 optional
candidates.quality_evidence: Seed-TTS-eval WER 1.24 (EN) for 1.7B-Base, speaker similarity 0.77-0.83 across languages (official). Newest major Apache-2.0 release (Jan 22, 2026), covered by Simon Willison and wide community adoption; 3-second rapid voice clone plus description-based voice design.

languages: 7 other languages incl. Japanese, Chinese, French, Hindi (secondary quality)
candidates.params_size: 82M; ~2-3 GB VRAM on GPU, runs on plain CPU; MLX/CoreML/ONNX ports are a few hundred MB
candidates.mac_feasibility: Best-in-class. mlx-audio, kokoro-mlx and a CoreML/ANE port all run it far faster than real time on Apple Silicon (M2 Ultra: 30 s of audio in 379 ms = 79x real time; MetalRT reports RTF ~0.0014 on short phrases). Trivial fit in 8 GB. The catch: fixed voice bank (54 voices), no reference-audio cloning — use it as the latency/quality-per-watt baseline, not the cloning pipeline.
candidates.install: pip install kokoro (PyTorch/ONNX) or pip install mlx-audio / kokoro-mlx on Mac
candidates.quality_evidence: ELO 1060 on Artificial Analysis speech arena (July 2026) — beats every commercial model priced under $15/1M chars; consensus 'best default open-source TTS' in 2026 roundups despite 82M params.
candidates.release_date: v1.0 2025-01-27 (still current as of mid-2026; no v2 released)

candidates.params_size: 0.5B (a 1.5B variant also exists); fp16 ~1.5 GB weights + codec, fits 8 GB; community GGUF quant available
candidates.mac_feasibility: Good. 0.5B fits easily in 8 GB; a GGUF port (cstr/cosyvoice3-0.5b-2512-GGUF) enables llama.cpp-style CPU/Metal inference; CosyVoice2 already ran on MPS in community setups. Expect near-real-time on M2 rather than the 3-4x real time seen on CUDA; fewer polished MLX ports than Chatterbox/Qwen3-TTS, so budget integration time.
candidates.install: git clone FunAudioLLM/CosyVoice + pip install -r requirements (PyTorch, works on CPU/MPS); or GGUF route via cstr/cosyvoice3-0.5b-2512-GGUF
candidates.quality_evidence: Official claim: surpasses CosyVoice 2 in content consistency, speaker similarity and prosody; trained on ~1M hours; strong zero-shot cloning lineage (CosyVoice2 was a 2025 cloning staple). Open-sourced 2025-12-15 with HF demo space.

candidates.params_size: 748M (Qwen 0.5B backbone + NeuCodec); GGUF Q4/Q8 ~0.5-1 GB — smallest serious cloning model
candidates.mac_feasibility: Excellent — designed for exactly this class of hardware. Ships in GGML/GGUF for llama.cpp/llama-cpp-python; community-reported RTF < 0.5 on CPU (Intel i5, even Raspberry Pi 5), ~50 tok/s; 3-15 s reference-audio cloning fully offline. Guaranteed to fit an M2 8GB without swap. Naturalness is good-but-not-frontier (community MOS estimates ~4.2 vs vendor claims; noticeably below Chatterbox on expressiveness).
candidates.install: pip install neutts-air (or llama-cpp-python with the GGUF from HF)
candidates.quality_evidence: MarkTechPost/HN coverage of the Oct 2025 release; positioned as best-in-class realism among on-device CPU models; instant cloning from ~3 s reference. Use as the safety-net cloning candidate if bigger models thrash on 8 GB.

candidates.model: Fish Speech / OpenAudio S1-mini and Fish Audio S2 (open-weights line)
candidates.license: NON-COMMERCIAL — flag: S1-mini weights are CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0; S2 weights are under the 'Fish Audio Research License' (free for research/non-commercial only; commercial use requires a paid license). Code is Apache-2.0/BSD.
candidates.params_size: S1-mini ~0.5B; S2 larger (undisclosed); S1-mini fits 8 GB, S2 likely tight
candidates.mac_feasibility: Moderate. fish-speech has macOS/MPS install docs and S1-mini (0.5B) fits 8 GB; expect around real-time or slower on a base M2 (Fish models are LLM+vocoder two-stage). No mature MLX port. The bigger blocker is the license, not the hardware.
candidates.install: git clone fishaudio/fish-speech, pip install -e . (has macOS instructions); S2 weights gated on HF
candidates.quality_evidence: Top of the open-weights quality pile: Fish Audio S2 Pro ELO 1110 (#2 open-weights, Artificial Analysis July 2026); S2 (Mar 2026) leads EmergentTTS-Eval/Seed-TTS Eval among open models, WER 0.99% EN on Seed-TTS Eval; Fish Speech V1.5 had ELO 1339 in older TTS-Arena. Include for benchmark comparison; exclude from anything commercial.

candidates.license: NON-COMMERCIAL weights — flag: code MIT, but pretrained weights CC-BY-NC-4.0 (trained on Emilia in-the-wild data)
candidates.params_size: ~336M DiT flow-matching model; ~1.5 GB runtime — fits 8 GB easily
candidates.mac_feasibility: Good fit, modest speed. Dedicated f5-tts-mlx port (pip install f5-tts-mlx): ~4 s to generate a sample on M3 Max, roughly ~1-2x real time on mid Apple Silicon — fine for offline benchmarking, not for low-latency streaming (non-autoregressive, no first-chunk streaming).
candidates.install: pip install f5-tts (PyTorch) or pip install f5-tts-mlx (Apple Silicon)
candidates.quality_evidence: Widely reproduced 2024-25 academic SOTA for zero-shot cloning fidelity (flow matching, very high speaker similarity); still a standard baseline in 2026 comparisons. Good WER/similarity but flat prosody vs 2026 LLM-based models.

candidates.params_size: 3B LLM-based; needs 12 GB VRAM (16 GB recommended); AWQ-4bit memory-efficient mode ~6-8 GB VRAM
candidates.mac_feasibility: Not realistic on M2 8GB — 3B audio LLM + codec with official NVIDIA-only requirements and no MLX port. Even AWQ-4bit (~6-8 GB) would swap on 8 GB unified memory once the codec and OS are resident. Treat as the Colab T4 (16 GB) stretch candidate: T4 fits the AWQ-4bit path.
candidates.install: git clone stepfun-ai/Step-Audio-EditX + pip install -r requirements.txt on CUDA; use stepfun-ai/Step-Audio-EditX-AWQ-4bit on T4
candidates.quality_evidence: #1 open-weights model on the Artificial Analysis speech arena (ELO 1118, July 2026 snapshot); zero-shot TTS plus unique iterative emotion/style/paralinguistic editing — useful for the naturalness (MOS) axis of the benchmark.

summary: As of July 2026, the Artificial Analysis speech arena's open-weights tier is led by Step-Audio-EditX (ELO 1118, Apache-2.0 but 12GB+ CUDA), Fish Audio S2 Pro (1110, non-commercial license), Kokoro-82M (1060), Maya1 (1053) and Chatterbox (1011-1123 depending on snapshot); no open model cracks the closed-source top 10, but the gap has narrowed to ~80 ELO. For an M2 8GB pipeline the strongest cloning-capable picks are Chatterbox/Chatterbox-Turbo (MIT, 350-500M, MLX fp16 ~1.3 GB, streaming fork with ~0.5 RTF and ~470 ms first-chunk on GPU, working Apple Silicon ports) and Qwen3-TTS-0.6B (Apache-2.0, Jan 2026, 3-s cloning, 97 ms streaming latency, MLX ports running in 2-3 GB RAM), with NeuTTS Air (748M, Apache-2.0, GGUF, RTF<0.5 on CPU) as the guaranteed-fit fallback and Kokoro-82M as the no-cloning speed/quality baseline (79x real time on M2 Ultra via MLX). CosyVoice3-0.5B (Apache-2.0, Dec 2025) is the best new zero-shot cloning research line that still fits 8 GB; F5-TTS and Fish/OpenAudio are worth benchmarking but carry CC-BY-NC(-SA)/research-only weight licenses, as do XTTS-v2 (Coqui CPML) and Mistral's Voxtral TTS 4B (CC-BY-NC, cloning API-only, 16 GB VRAM). CUDA-only/T4-stretch candidates to keep separately: Step-Audio-EditX (AWQ-4bit fits a T4), Maya1 3B (Apache-2.0 but description-based voice design rather than reference cloning), and IndexTTS-2 (weights require a commercial license from Bilibili). Older 2025 names (Dia, Orpheus, Zonos ELO 1000, Sesame CSM, Parler, Piper, VibeVoice 7B ELO 958) are now outranked on naturalness or lack practical 8GB cloning paths.

candidates.license: Code MIT; MSA/ALG/EGY/IRQ/MAR checkpoints Apache-2.0; Unified/SAU/UAE checkpoints CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 (dataset-inherited)
candidates.params_size: F5-TTS DiT backbone (~330M params class); trained 200K updates on 8xH100; checkpoint ~1.3-1.5 GB, fits in 8GB unified memory
candidates.install: pip install habibi-tts (then habibi-tts_infer-gradio or CLI with --dialect flag); inherits F5-TTS install path which supports MPS
candidates.mac_feasibility: Good fit: F5-TTS runs on Apple Silicon MPS; community reports RTF ~0.15 on M3 Max/M4 Pro, expect roughly RTF 1-3 (slower than realtime) on M2 8GB but memory-safe (~2-3 GB runtime). Reduce NFE steps to trade quality for speed.
candidates.quality_evidence: arXiv 2601.13802 (Jan 2026): first standardized multi-dialect Arabic TTS benchmark (11k+ utterances, 7 dialect sets); beats ElevenLabs Eleven v3 (alpha) on most dialect WER (e.g. UAE 5.33 vs 7.61, EGY 14.96 vs 17.60, MAR 30.13 vs 35.79; MSA 7.83 vs 6.77 slightly behind) and dominates speaker similarity (UAE 0.861 vs 0.615). Zero-shot cloning from reference audio. Works on RAW UNDIACRITIZED text — no tashkeel pipeline needed.

candidates.model: Chatterbox Multilingual (Resemble AI) — YES, Arabic shipped
candidates.params_size: 0.5B Llama backbone + S3Gen decoder; community reports ~4 GB VRAM for generation; total runtime ~5-6 GB
candidates.install: pip install chatterbox-tts (use ChatterboxMultilingualTTS class, language_id='ar'); Multilingual v3 released with improved speaker similarity
candidates.mac_feasibility: Borderline on M2 8GB: runs on Apple Silicon (community MPS port Jimmi42/chatterbox-tts-apple-silicon, 2-3x speedup claimed) but has documented MPS tensor issues forcing CPU fallback and a memory leak (~222-800MB per few generations, GitHub issue #218). Feasible for batch generation with process restarts; not for long sessions. Strong Colab T4 candidate.
candidates.quality_evidence: Resemble confirms Arabic in the 23-language launch (Sept 2025) with cross-lingual cloning ('switch from English to Arabic and the speaker still sounds like themselves'); benchmarked preferred over ElevenLabs in blind tests (English); Wiro AI review reports 'strong' Arabic clone quality at ~4GB VRAM; community Arabic LoRA fine-tune exists (juliardi/chatterbox-multilingual-finetuned-arabic, MSA-focused). Arabic-specific MOS numbers not published — verify by ear.

candidates.license: CPML 1.0 — NON-COMMERCIAL weights (Coqui shut down Jan 2024, no commercial license obtainable); library code MPL-2.0
candidates.params_size: ~750M params, ~1.9 GB checkpoint; ~4 GB RAM at inference
candidates.install: pip install coqui-tts; tts_model 'tts_models/multilingual/multi-dataset/xtts_v2', language='ar'
candidates.mac_feasibility: Runs CPU-only on Mac (MPS broken — coqui-ai/TTS issue #3649); RTF several times slower than realtime on M2 but fits 8GB. Fine for offline benchmark generation, not low-latency.
candidates.quality_evidence: Arabic natively in the 16/17-language pretraining (Interspeech 2024 paper, arXiv 2406.04904); training data skews MSA+Gulf, Egyptian underrepresented. Known caveat: pronunciation degrades on undiacritized text — community pipelines run Mishkal/CATT tashkeel first (e.g. Moeeldouma/arabic-tts-xtts-v2 wraps Mishkal with --tashkeel flag and 166-char chunking). 6-second zero-shot cloning. Still the most battle-tested Arabic cloner despite age.

candidates.params_size: 0.5B distilled from 4B S1; ~2-3 GB runtime with codec; docs cite 12GB min for larger models but S1-mini is far smaller
candidates.install: git clone fish-speech repo + follow install docs (pip install -e .), download openaudio-s1-mini weights; Docker image available
candidates.mac_feasibility: Officially supported: fish-speech added Apple Silicon MPS support with dedicated bug fixes and reference-audio caching (changelog + GitHub issue #356). Autoregressive LLM decoding means RTF well below realtime on M2; memory OK at 8GB. Better on T4.
candidates.quality_evidence: Trained on 2M hours; S1 series took top ranks on TTS-Arena-style evals for English/Chinese; Arabic is explicitly tier-2 with no phoneme dependency (raw text in). Arabic-specific community MOS evidence is thin — treat as 'supported but unproven for MSA', verify with round-trip WER. Emotion/tone control markers work across languages.

candidates.license: CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 + Llama 3.2 Community License (non-commercial in practice); check 0.6B variant (Apache-2.0, 14 langs) for Arabic inclusion before relying on it
candidates.params_size: 1B params; GGUF Q4 ~700MB-1GB, FP16 ~2GB — the lightest cloning-capable Arabic option
candidates.install: pip install outetts (llama.cpp backend recommended on Mac) or use GGUF directly with llama-server
candidates.mac_feasibility: Best M2 8GB fit of all cloning models: llama.cpp/GGUF native path runs comfortably in <2GB, near-realtime on M2. Automatic word alignment means raw Arabic text in (numbers/mixed scripts handled internally).
candidates.quality_evidence: Arabic listed in the highest training-data tier (blog: outeai.com/blog/outetts-1-0-release); one-shot cloning from ~10s audio. No published Arabic MOS or arena ranking; overall naturalness generally rated below Chatterbox/F5-class models by the community — solid mid-tier, great efficiency story.

candidates.params_size: SpeechT5-class ~155M + HiFi-GAN vocoder; <1 GB total — trivially fits 8GB
candidates.install: transformers (SpeechT5ForTextToSpeech from MBZUAI/speecht5_tts_clartts_ar) or the ArTST repo (fairseq-based) for v2 checkpoints
candidates.mac_feasibility: Excellent: SpeechT5-size runs realtime-or-faster on M2 CPU/MPS via transformers. Lowest-risk local option.
candidates.quality_evidence: Peer-reviewed (ArTSTv2/v3 at ACL main; ClArTTS paper arXiv 2303.00069); the definitive Arabic-first speech stack from MBZUAI. Paper finding: reasonable TTS WITHOUT diacritics. Caveats: TTS checkpoint is single-male-speaker Classical Arabic (12h audiobook) — clean and correct but noticeably less natural than 2025-26 LLM-TTS; speaker conditioning via x-vector/ECAPA embeddings, not true zero-shot cloning. Good as an Arabic-native baseline, not a naturalness winner.

candidates.license: Non-commercial (research/educational/personal only; commercial needs author permission)
candidates.params_size: F5-TTS architecture (~330M class), single .pt checkpoint ~1.3 GB
candidates.install: F5-TTS CLI (pip install f5-tts) pointing --ckpt/--vocab at the HF files
candidates.mac_feasibility: Same as F5/Habibi: MPS-capable, ~2-3 GB runtime, RTF ~1-3 on M2 8GB. Fine.
candidates.quality_evidence: Fine-tuned on ~300h clean MSA audio; predecessor trained on ClArTTS (see SWivid/F5-TTS discussion #782). HARD REQUIREMENT: input must be fully diacritized (tashkeel) — pair with CATT or Mishkal. Community-maintained, no formal MOS; largely superseded by Habibi-TTS (same architecture, bigger data, no-tashkeel, Apache MSA checkpoint) but useful as an MSA-specialist A/B comparison.

candidates.params_size: 36.3M params (~145 MB) — smallest by far
candidates.install: transformers >=4.33: VitsModel.from_pretrained('facebook/mms-tts-ara')
candidates.mac_feasibility: Trivial: faster-than-realtime on M2 CPU, negligible memory. Zero-risk floor model.
candidates.quality_evidence: Single fixed voice, no cloning, no dialect control; from the MMS 1000+-language project (arXiv 2305.13516) trained largely on religious-domain recordings — intelligible MSA but flat/robotic prosody; community consensus places it well below XTTS/Chatterbox/Habibi on naturalness. Use strictly as latency/WER baseline in the benchmark table, not as a headline pipeline.

summary: The claim that natural-sounding open TTS skips Arabic is now outdated: Habibi-TTS (Jan 2026, from the F5-TTS team, arXiv 2601.13802) is a purpose-built open Arabic model with an Apache-2.0 MSA checkpoint plus 11 dialect variants, zero-shot cloning, no tashkeel required, and published benchmarks beating ElevenLabs v3 (alpha) on most dialect WER and speaker-similarity sets — it is the clear primary pick and fits an M2 8GB via F5's MPS path. Chatterbox Multilingual did ship Arabic (MIT, 23 languages, Sept 2025, cross-lingual cloning) and is the best permissively-licensed fallback, though on 8GB Macs it suffers MPS bugs and a memory leak, making it a stronger Colab T4 candidate; XTTS-v2 still works for Arabic cloning but is non-commercial (CPML) and needs a diacritization front-end. Note Qwen3-TTS (open-sourced Jan 2026) does NOT officially support Arabic — only a community KSA fine-tune exists — and OpenAudio S1-mini lists Arabic only as tier-2 with thin evidence. On diacritization: Habibi and ArTST accept raw undiacritized text, while XTTS-v2 and the Arabic F5 fine-tunes need full tashkeel — use CATT (github.com/abjadai/catt, SOTA DER, beats GPT-4-turbo) or Mishkal (rule-based, what community XTTS pipelines use); CAMeL Tools (MIT) and Farasa (API, research license) are alternatives. Recommended benchmark lineup on M2 8GB: Habibi-TTS (primary), OuteTTS-1B GGUF (efficiency/cloning), ArTST (Arabic-native academic baseline), MMS-TTS-ara (floor), with Chatterbox Multilingual added on T4.

candidates.model: mlx-audio (Blaizzy) — umbrella MLX TTS runtime for Apple Silicon
languages: depends on model: EN/AR/HI covered via Chatterbox-multilingual; HI via Kokoro; 100+ via Higgs v3/OmniVoice
candidates.install: pip install mlx-audio (Python 3.10+, Apple Silicon); models pulled from mlx-community on HF
candidates.params_size: runtime itself is light; supports 3/4/6/8-bit quantized model conversions
candidates.mac_feasibility: The primary recommended runtime for M2 8GB. As of mid-2026 it supports ~17 TTS families: Kokoro, KittenTTS, Qwen3-TTS, Higgs Audio v3, OmniVoice, CSM/MisoTTS, Dia, OuteTTS, Spark-TTS, Chatterbox (23-lang multilingual), Soprano, Ming Omni, KugelAudio, Voxtral, LongCat-AudioDiT, MeloTTS, MOSS-TTS. Has a --stream CLI flag; stream mode yields audio sentence-by-sentence for lower TTFA. MLX's unified-memory/lazy-eval design is what keeps 0.5-1B models inside 8GB where PyTorch swaps (community 8GB report: MLX peak ~6GB vs PyTorch ~10GB for a 0.6B TTS). Repo publishes no official RTF table — benchmark per-model.
candidates.quality_evidence: Model list and quantization confirmed from README; independent CoreML comparison found mlx-audio Kokoro ~2x slower than a CoreML port but still many-times real-time on M-series

candidates.install: pip install mlx-audio then --model mlx-community/Kokoro-82M-bf16 (also bf16/8/6/4-bit); or kokoro-onnx with CoreML EP
candidates.params_size: 82M params; ~900MB peak RAM reported; bf16 weights ~330MB
candidates.mac_feasibility: Best-in-class on 8GB M2: an M-series 2026 bench reports RTF 0.08 and ~90ms TTFA (M5 Max; expect a few x slower on M2 but still well under real-time); an 8GB M1 Air report: 1,500 words in ~30-45s. CoreML ports hit 12-79x real-time across M-chips. Safest EN+HI 'naturalness baseline' model; no cloning.
candidates.quality_evidence: Consistently top-ranked small model for naturalness in 2025-26 community roundups; used as the default local TTS in many Mac guides

candidates.model: Chatterbox Multilingual — MLX 4-bit port (the key EN+AR+HI cloning candidate for 8GB Mac)
candidates.license: MIT upstream (Resemble AI); MLX conversion card lists Apache-2.0
languages: 23 languages incl. English, Arabic, Hindi, plus FR/DE/ES/ZH/JA/KO etc.
candidates.install: pip install -U mlx-audio; python -m mlx_audio.tts.generate --model mlx-community/chatterbox-4bit --text ... (ref_audio + transcript for cloning; lang_code per language)
candidates.params_size: 0.5B backbone; 4-bit MLX weights ~607MB (card shows 0.2B/607MB for the quantized artifact); also mlx-community/chatterbox-turbo-4bit (350M-class turbo variant)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Feasible on M2 8GB thanks to 4-bit MLX weights — this sidesteps the PyTorch-on-Mac problems (MPS tensor errors, ~222-800MB/gen memory leak in resemble-ai/chatterbox issue #218). No published M2 RTF yet — benchmark it; expect slower than Kokoro but tractable. This is the only single model covering EN+AR+HI with zero-shot cloning that plausibly fits 8GB.
candidates.quality_evidence: Chatterbox family is a top open-weights arena performer; v3 claims RTF ~5x real-time on H100 and Turbo ~75ms latency (server GPUs, not Mac). Outputs carry Perth watermark.
candidates.release_date: multilingual v2/v3 late 2025 - 2026; MLX conversion via mlx-audio 0.2.7

candidates.license: MIT (port code); F5-TTS base weights CC-BY-NC-4.0 (non-commercial)
languages: NO Arabic/Hindi in base (community finetunes like IndicF5 exist but are PyTorch)
candidates.install: pip install f5-tts-mlx; --q 4|8 for quantized weights; ref audio = mono 24kHz wav, 5-10s + transcript
candidates.params_size: ~336M DiT + Vocos; fp16 ~1.3GB, 4/8-bit smaller — fits 8GB comfortably
candidates.mac_feasibility: Works but NOT real-time on base M-chips: ~4s per short sample on M3 Max; RTF ~0.94 reported on M2 Max; one M-series report of 63s for a 50s clip (RTF ~1.26). On plain M2 expect around/above RTF 1. Non-autoregressive, so no incremental streaming — TTFA equals full-utterance latency.
candidates.quality_evidence: F5-TTS is a widely-cited zero-shot cloning SOTA (paper RTF 0.15 on high-end GPU); strong speaker-similarity reputation

candidates.install: pip install git+https://github.com/senstella/csm-mlx (Python <3.13); nn.quantize() for 4/8-bit
candidates.params_size: 1B; quantized fits 8GB
candidates.mac_feasibility: Explicitly documented: quantization gets it 'nearly real-time on M2 Air' (with some quality loss) — directly relevant to M2 8GB. Voice cloning via conversation context (audio+text+speaker id). Also exposed inside mlx-audio as CSM/MisoTTS.
candidates.quality_evidence: Sesame's conversational-speech demo generated major buzz in 2025; stream_generate() API with configurable chunk accumulation

candidates.license: Coqui Public Model License (CPML) — non-commercial for the model weights
candidates.install: pip install coqui-tts (idiap fork; macOS wheels since 0.24.2, Python 3.10-3.12)
candidates.params_size: ~460M; 4-6GB VRAM on GPU; ~4.5GB peak RAM reported on Mac
candidates.mac_feasibility: Marginal on M2 8GB: MPS support is historically broken (coqui-ai/TTS issue #3649 — hangs on MPS; CPU works), though one 2026 M5 Max report ran it on MPS at RTF 0.34, ~600ms TTFA, ~4.5GB peak. On an 8GB machine that peak plus OS is right at the swap line; run CPU/fp16, short texts, nothing else open. Shines on T4 instead: ~200ms to first streamed chunk, RTF ~0.25 (Baseten). Covers EN+AR+HI with cloning — good T4 benchmark anchor, license is non-commercial.
candidates.quality_evidence: Baseten production writeup (T4 streaming); HF discussion #10 (212ms first-chunk demo); Contra Collective 2026 Mac bench
candidates.release_date: XTTS-v2 Nov 2023; idiap fork actively maintained through 2026

candidates.model: Chatterbox (PyTorch) on Mac — Jimmi42 / lorenjphillips MPS forks
candidates.install: clone fork + pip install chatterbox-tts with device-mapping patches (torch.load map_location for CUDA-trained weights)
candidates.params_size: 0.5B backbone; fp32/fp16 PyTorch footprint several GB
candidates.mac_feasibility: NOT recommended on 8GB: forks advertise 2-3x speedup on MPS but real reports are mixed (M3 Studio 'speed only double'), MPS tensor incompatibilities force CPU fallback in the Jimmi42 app, and upstream has a critical memory leak (~222-800MB per few generations, issue #218) — a swap-death recipe on 8GB. Use the MLX 4-bit port instead; keep this entry only as evidence of PyTorch-on-Mac pain.
candidates.quality_evidence: Community T4/streaming reports: advertised ~200ms latency but 'takes 2-3x longer in practice'; streaming ~500ms-1s TTFA (issue #193)

candidates.install: conda env per repo; CPU on macOS; MPS support only recently merged (PR #1869, Apr 2026) — verify current state
candidates.params_size: 0.5B LLM + flow-matching decoder; several GB in PyTorch
candidates.mac_feasibility: CPU-only on Mac until the Apr-2026 MPS PR; a zero-shot generation took ~20s on M2 16GB CPU (issue #894); community RTF ~0.3-0.5 on M-series with ~30s first-run warmup. On 8GB it is risky (PyTorch footprint) and it lacks Arabic/Hindi, so mainly a T4 streaming-latency reference: paper claims 150ms first-packet in streaming mode on GPU.
candidates.quality_evidence: CosyVoice2 paper (arXiv 2412.10117) — bidirectional streaming design, 150ms first-packet claim; strong MOS in paper

candidates.install: T4 path: pip install from repo, fp16; Mac path: mlx-audio Dia support (quantize to 4/8-bit)
candidates.params_size: 1.6B; ~4.4GB VRAM at fp16, ~7.9GB at fp32, ~10GB recommended
candidates.mac_feasibility: Marginal on 8GB M2 even via MLX (1.6B bf16 ~3.2GB weights + runtime + OS); only try 4-bit. PyTorch upstream targets CUDA. Better treated as a Colab T4 candidate: fp16 fits T4 comfortably. EN-only and dialogue-styled, so low priority for this case study.
candidates.quality_evidence: Viral April-2025 release for two-speaker dialogue realism; voice conditioning via audio prompt

candidates.license: Llama 3.2 Community License + CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 (OuteAI additions) — non-commercial
languages: 23 languages; high tier: EN/FR/DE/ES/ZH/JA/RU; Arabic listed in supported set, Hindi not in high tier — verify per-language quality
candidates.install: llama.cpp (brew/curl) + GGUF quants 591MB-1.33GB; or outetts pip package; also OuteTTS in mlx-audio
candidates.params_size: 1B; GGUF q-variants 0.6-1.3GB — trivially fits 8GB
candidates.mac_feasibility: Good fit memory-wise: llama.cpp Metal is battle-tested on 8GB Macs; 10s reference audio for cloning; 8k context caps a run at ~42s of audio. Token-rate is 150 audio tokens/s of content — generation speed depends on chip; llama.cpp gives most reliable output quality per OuteAI. No true incremental audio streaming pipeline out of the box.
candidates.quality_evidence: OuteAI model card (sampling: temp 0.4, rep-pen 1.1); community: 'llama.cpp delivers the most reliable and consistent output quality'

candidates.model: Qwen3-TTS-0.6B-Base 8-bit via MLX (proof that cloning works on 8GB)
languages: and more per Qwen3-TTS card — Arabic/Hindi unverified
candidates.install: mlx-audio with Qwen3-TTS-12Hz-0.6B-Base-8bit (avoid PyTorch path entirely on 8GB)
candidates.params_size: 0.6B; 8-bit MLX peak ~6GB total; PyTorch same model peaked ~10GB (swap death on 8GB)
candidates.mac_feasibility: The best-documented 8GB datapoint found: on an 8GB M1, 8-bit MLX ran at RTF 0.54 (faster than real-time) with ~6GB peak; 8-bit was both faster and cleaner than 4-bit; 15s reference clip + exact Whisper transcript for cloning; MLX Metal backend crashes under concurrent GPU ops — serialize requests. The 1.7B CustomVoice variant needs 16GB+.
candidates.quality_evidence: Detailed first-person 8GB benchmark write-up with memory/RTF numbers; Qwen3-TTS is a strong 2025-26 multilingual family
candidates.release_date: Qwen3-TTS open weights late 2025; MLX ports 2026

candidates.model: Higgs Audio v3 TTS (4B, Boson AI) — T4/stretch tier only
languages: ~100 languages incl. Arabic and Hindi, with voice cloning
candidates.install: SGLang-Omni serving (CUDA); also listed in mlx-audio model zoo but 4.7B is impractical on 8GB
candidates.params_size: ~4.7B (Qwen3-4B backbone); ~10GB VRAM fp16, ~2.5GB INT4 (estimates)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Not viable on M2 8GB (even 4-bit weights + KV cache + OS leave no headroom, and no community 8GB success reports). On Colab T4 16GB fp16 (~10GB) fits — the strongest 'one model, all three languages + cloning' option for the T4 stretch goal; real-time serving numbers published on H100-class via SGLang, expect well below that on T4.
candidates.quality_evidence: LMSYS/SGLang June-2026 blog: beats other open models incl. Chatterbox and MOSS-TTS on their eval; 8k context, 24kHz output

summary: On an M2 with 8GB, MLX is effectively the only safe runtime for cloning-capable TTS: a documented 8GB run of a 0.6B model showed PyTorch peaking ~10GB (swap death) vs ~6GB peak and RTF 0.54 under 8-bit MLX, so the plan should be mlx-audio + quantized mlx-community weights, one model resident at a time, with serialized generation (MLX Metal crashes on concurrent ops). Concretely: Kokoro-82M covers English+Hindi at RTF ~0.08-0.5 and ~900MB (no cloning); mlx-community/chatterbox-4bit (607MB, 23 languages incl. Arabic and Hindi, zero-shot cloning) is the single 8GB-viable model covering all three case-study languages and needs first-party benchmarking since no M2 RTF is published; f5-tts-mlx (EN/ZH, ~RTF 1 on M2-class, no streaming) and csm-mlx ("nearly real-time on M2 Air" quantized, EN) are secondary cloning options. PyTorch paths degrade on Mac: XTTS-v2 MPS historically hangs (CPU works, ~4.5GB peak), Chatterbox PyTorch has MPS tensor bugs plus a 222-800MB/generation memory leak, and CosyVoice2 was CPU-only until an Apr-2026 MPS PR. Streaming TTFA: on-Mac expect ~90ms (Kokoro, sentence-chunked) to 600ms+ (XTTS); on a Colab T4, XTTS-v2 streams first audio in ~200ms at RTF ~0.25 (best documented T4 number), Chatterbox streams at ~500ms-1s in practice, CosyVoice2 claims 150ms first-packet, and Dia-1.6B (fp16 ~4.4GB) plus Higgs Audio v3 4B (~10GB fp16, ~100 languages incl. AR/HI with cloning) become viable only on the T4 tier.

languages: English-dominant training (100k+ MOS-labeled clips: VoiceMOS 2022, NISQA, PSTN, ConferencingSpeech, TTS outputs); not validated for Arabic/Hindi
candidates.install: pip install distillmos (clean PyPI wheel; torch-only, no fairseq). Usage: distillmos.ConvTransformerSQAModel(), 16 kHz mono tensor in, MOS 1-5 out
candidates.params_size: Compact student model, ~2 orders of magnitude smaller than its wav2vec2 XLS-R teacher (tens of MB); trivial RAM footprint
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes - designed for efficiency; CPU inference on M2 8GB is comfortable (seconds per clip). This is the recommended default MOS predictor for the harness
candidates.quality_evidence: Paper (arXiv 2502.05356, Feb 2025) shows distillation halves the correlation gap to the XLS-R teacher on held-out MOS datasets; adopted into ClearerVoice-Studio SpeechScore as DISTILL_MOS in May 2025. Caveat: like all VoiceMOS-era predictors, correlation drops out-of-domain; treat Arabic/Hindi scores as proxies and always report delta vs real-speech anchors in the same language rather than absolute cross-language comparisons

candidates.license: MIT (re-export of official sarulab-speech UTMOS22 checkpoint)
languages: English (BVCC / VoiceMOS 2022 main track). Validity for non-English formally NOT established - disclose this for Arabic and Hindi
candidates.install: No pip package needed: predictor = torch.hub.load("tarepan/SpeechMOS:v1.2.0", "utmos22_strong", trust_repo=True). Needs only torch+torchaudio - avoids the notorious fairseq install breakage of the official UTMOS22 repo (fairseq 0.12.2 has omegaconf/hydra conflicts on modern pip)
candidates.params_size: wav2vec2-base backbone, ~95M params (~400MB fp32); fine in 8GB RAM
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes - CPU inference at roughly a few seconds per 10s clip on M2. Include it because UTMOS is the score every TTS paper reports, so it makes your numbers comparable to published claims
candidates.quality_evidence: VoiceMOS 2022 winning system; still the most-cited automatic naturalness metric in TTS papers (used by F5-TTS etc.). Known caveats: trained/validated on English; cross-lingual comparisons unreliable (UTMOS 'optimized for English' per Cross-Lingual F5-TTS paper); scores for Arabic/Hindi are approximate proxies only

candidates.install: pip install git+https://github.com/sarulab-speech/UTMOSv2.git (supports Python 3.9-3.13); then model.predict(input_path=...). Git-install only, no PyPI wheel
candidates.params_size: SSL (wav2vec2) + EfficientNetV2 spectrogram branch fusion - larger and slower than UTMOS/Distill-MOS
candidates.mac_feasibility: Runs on CPU but noticeably slower than Distill-MOS/UTMOS; acceptable for a few hundred clips, treat as optional third opinion
candidates.quality_evidence: 1st place in 7/16 and 2nd in 9/16 metrics at VoiceMOS Challenge 2024 Track 1 (SLT 2024) - best at discriminating between already-good modern TTS systems, which is exactly your regime; but English-centric like UTMOS

candidates.license: Code MIT; pretrained weights CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (non-commercial - fine for a case study, flag it)
languages: Trained on Blizzard/VCC synthetic-speech naturalness data; reported to generalize across target languages better than UTMOS (multilingual mixture)
candidates.install: git clone https://github.com/gabrielmittag/NISQA + create env (official env.yml pins Python 3.9; a plain 'pip install librosa pandas torch...' venv also works); run: python run_predict.py --mode predict_dir --pretrained_model weights/nisqa_tts.tar --data_dir wavs/ --output_dir results/. Unofficial 'pip install nisqa' exists on PyPI but ships without weights
candidates.params_size: Small CNN-self-attention model (<10M params); trivial on CPU
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes - fast CPU inference, the lightest of the MOS predictors. Main friction is the clunky clone+weights workflow, not compute
candidates.quality_evidence: The Cross-Lingual F5-TTS paper explicitly chose NISQA over UTMOS because 'UTMOS is optimized for English, whereas NISQA-MOS uses a multilingual mixture, generalizing better across target languages' - useful as your Arabic/Hindi-facing predictor. Note IndicMOS (Interspeech 2024) still found ALL open-source MOS predictors degrade on Indian languages, so pair every predicted-MOS number with real-speech anchors per language

candidates.model: torchaudio SQUIM_SUBJECTIVE (tertiary MOS, use with caveats)
languages: English training data (DNS-type corpora); designed for degraded/transmitted speech more than TTS naturalness
candidates.install: Already inside torchaudio (pip install torchaudio) - zero extra deps. torchaudio entered maintenance phase at 2.8 (Aug 2025) with some APIs removed in 2.9, but models/pipelines including SQUIM were retained; pin your torchaudio version in requirements
candidates.params_size: Small; CPU-trivial
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes, but two structural problems for TTS benchmarking: (1) SQUIM-MOS requires a Non-Matching Reference clip of real speech per estimate, unlike UTMOS/NISQA/Distill-MOS which are truly blind; (2) documented bias toward scoring short clips disproportionately higher (TTSDS2). Use only as a cross-check, not headline metric
candidates.quality_evidence: TTSDS2 (2025) found SQUIM-MOS among the metrics correlating best with subjective TTS ratings in some domains, but also flagged the short-clip bias; VoiceMOS analyses show all such predictors drop correlation out-of-domain

languages: Trained on VoxCeleb1+2 (English-dominant but multi-accent). Works cross-lingually for Arabic/Hindi, but absolute similarity values shift with language mismatch - calibrate per language
candidates.install: pip install speechbrain (clean PyPI). from speechbrain.inference.speaker import SpeakerRecognition; model = SpeakerRecognition.from_hparams('speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb'); score, decision = model.verify_files(ref.wav, gen.wav)
candidates.params_size: ~20M params, <100MB download; 192-dim embeddings
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes - fast CPU inference (well under 1s per utterance pair on M2). Recommended primary SIM metric: best trade-off of install cleanliness, discriminative power (VoxCeleb EER ~0.8%), and ubiquity in TTS papers
candidates.quality_evidence: CRITICAL threshold point for the report: cosine scores are NOT comparable across embedding models. SpeechBrain ECAPA's verify_batch ships with default decision threshold 0.25 on raw cosine (-1..1); genuine same-speaker pairs typically land ~0.4-0.7 and different speakers ~0.0-0.25. By contrast microsoft/wavlm-base-plus-sv's model card uses threshold 0.86, and Resemblyzer GE2E same-speaker sims sit ~0.75-0.9+ - so a blanket '0.75 = same speaker' is meaningless unless you name the model. Also disclose: verification-optimized embeddings correlate only moderately with human-perceived similarity (VoxSim reports ~0.73-0.77 for ECAPA/WavLM-ECAPA). Best practice: report cosine(ref, generated) alongside a same-speaker baseline (two different real clips of the reference speaker) and a different-speaker baseline from your own data, per language

languages: Pretrained on 94k h English-heavy audio (Libri-Light/GigaSpeech/VoxPopuli), fine-tuned on VoxCeleb1
candidates.install: pip install transformers torchaudio; use Wav2Vec2FeatureExtractor + WavLMForXVector, cosine over x-vectors
candidates.params_size: ~95M backbone + x-vector head; heavier and slower on CPU than ECAPA
candidates.mac_feasibility: Runs on M2 CPU but several times slower than ECAPA; use only if you want a second SIM opinion or to mirror zero-shot-TTS papers that report WavLM-based SIM
candidates.quality_evidence: Model card demo threshold is 0.86 cosine, explicitly noted as dataset-dependent - a concrete illustration that thresholds do not transfer across embedding models (vs ECAPA's 0.25 default). Community comparisons found HF wavlm-base-plus-sv weaker at speaker retrieval than ECAPA (top-1 53% vs 97.6% in one 2025 study), so prefer ECAPA as primary

candidates.model: WeSpeaker ResNet34 / Resemblyzer (SIM alternatives - not recommended as primary)
candidates.install: WeSpeaker: pip install git+https://github.com/wenet-e2e/wespeaker.git (git-only; CLI 'wespeaker --task embedding', model.set_gpu(-1) for CPU). Resemblyzer: pip install resemblyzer (easiest install of all, 256-dim GE2E)
candidates.params_size: WeSpeaker ResNet34 ~25M; Resemblyzer ~1.4M (tiny)
candidates.mac_feasibility: Both CPU-fine on M2. WeSpeaker is production-grade but git-install + CLI friction; Resemblyzer is essentially unmaintained (v0.1.x, GE2E-era 2019 tech) and the least discriminative embedding in recent comparisons (top-1 retrieval 78% vs ECAPA 97.6%) - its same-speaker cosines cluster high (~0.75-0.9), which inflates apparent similarity of mediocre clones
candidates.quality_evidence: Include at most as sanity checks; do not mix their cosine numbers with ECAPA numbers in one table

candidates.model: faster-whisper large-v3-turbo int8 (primary round-trip WER ASR, all 3 languages)
languages: Arabic (MSA: near-human FLEURS WER ~4-8% zero-shot for large-v3)
Hindi (weaker: large-v3 ~19% avg WER across FLEURS/MLS/CV - hence the Indic cross-check below)
candidates.install: pip install faster-whisper; WhisperModel('large-v3-turbo', device='cpu', compute_type='int8'). CTranslate2 backend, clean PyPI
candidates.params_size: turbo = 809M params; int8 ~1.5GB RAM - the practical ceiling for an 8GB M2 alongside a TTS model; full large-v3 int8 (~1.6B params) also fits but roughly halves throughput
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes - community reports ~4-5x real-time on Apple Silicon for turbo int8 with ~1.5GB memory; this is the recommended round-trip ASR on the Mac. Run full large-v3 fp16 on the Colab T4 as a cross-check, since turbo's accuracy drop is largest on lower-resource languages
candidates.quality_evidence: large-v3 reduced errors 10-20% vs large-v2 across Common Voice 15/FLEURS; turbo benchmarked ~5x faster with near-identical English accuracy. WER protocol: compute WER with jiwer after language-specific normalization (see camel-tools and indic-nlp entries); report CER alongside WER for Arabic and Hindi
candidates.release_date: large-v3 Nov 2023, turbo Oct 2024, faster-whisper maintained through 2026

candidates.install: brew install whisper-cpp (or cmake build); download ggml-large-v3-turbo-q5_0.bin; runs on the M2 GPU via Metal using unified memory
candidates.params_size: q5_0 quantization shrinks large-v3 from ~1.5GB to ~1.1GB with negligible WER loss; turbo q5_0 smaller still
candidates.mac_feasibility: Excellent - ~10x real-time on M-series with Metal; best raw speed on the Mac. Trade-off: batch scoring pipelines are easier in Python with faster-whisper; use whisper.cpp if the eval harness becomes ASR-bound
candidates.quality_evidence: mac-whisper-speedtest benchmarks show whisper.cpp large-v3-turbo-q5_0 with CoreML among fastest Apple Silicon configs (~1.2s for the test clip)

candidates.model: AI4Bharat IndicConformer-600M / IndicWhisper (Hindi WER cross-check ASR)
candidates.install: IndicConformer: AutoModel.from_pretrained('ai4bharat/indic-conformer-600m-multilingual', trust_remote_code=True), CTC or RNNT decoding, explicit CPU fallback in example code (no NeMo needed for inference). IndicWhisper: standard transformers Whisper checkpoint from the Vistaar repo
candidates.params_size: 600M params (~2.4GB fp32) - fits on 8GB if the TTS model is unloaded first; run ASR as a separate pass
candidates.mac_feasibility: Yes on CPU (slow but batch-tolerant). Motivation: vanilla Whisper large-v3 Hindi WER ~19% would dominate your round-trip metric with ASR error, masking TTS intelligibility differences. IndicWhisper had lowest WER on 39/59 Vistaar benchmarks (avg -4.1 WER vs best public alternatives); report Hindi round-trip WER with BOTH whisper-turbo and an Indic model
candidates.quality_evidence: Vistaar (Interspeech 2023) is the standard Hindi ASR benchmark suite; IndicConformer is AI4Bharat's current 22-language flagship

candidates.install: pip install camel-tools (PyPI, v1.5.x). Normalization pipeline for WER: camel_tools.utils.dediac.dediac_ar (strip all diacritics/tashkeel) + camel_tools.utils.normalize.normalize_alef_ar (أ/إ/آ → ا), normalize_alef_maksura_ar (ى → ي), normalize_teh_marbuta_ar (ة → ه); then remove punctuation and compute WER with jiwer. Lighter alternative: pip install pyarabic (strip_tashkeel etc.)
candidates.params_size: Pure-Python text utils; negligible
candidates.mac_feasibility: Trivially yes. Protocol note: this alef/hamza/taa-marbuta + dediacritization recipe is the standard normalization in Arabic ASR papers (undiacritized WER); Whisper outputs mostly undiacritized MSA while references may be diacritized, so unnormalized WER is misleadingly high. Report BOTH raw and normalized WER, plus CER
candidates.quality_evidence: Alif/Hamza unification, Ta-Marbuta/Ha unification and diacritic removal are documented as the standard Arabic normalization ops (AraToken 2025, QCRI Arabic Normalizer); diacritized vs undiacritized WER/CER are reported separately in Arabic ASR literature

candidates.install: pip install indic-nlp-library whisper-normalizer jiwer. Use IndicNormalizerFactory().get_normalizer('hi') for Unicode NFC + nukta/conjunct canonicalization on both reference and hypothesis, then jiwer for WER/CER/MER
candidates.params_size: Pure Python; negligible
candidates.mac_feasibility: Trivially yes. CRITICAL pitfall to disclose: do NOT run OpenAI Whisper's BasicTextNormalizer on Devanagari - it strips matras (vowel diacritics) and mangles the text, producing garbage WER numbers; whisper-normalizer implements Indic-safe normalization derived from indic-nlp-library. Devanagari conjuncts can be encoded as different codepoint sequences that render identically, so skipping normalization inflates WER
candidates.quality_evidence: Documented standard practice for Indic ASR eval: indic-nlp normalization → jiwer WER+CER (Sarvam AI eval guide, kavyamanohar.com normalization series, SN-WER paper 2026); pair WER with CER since Hindi WER is inflated by tokenization/sandhi effects

candidates.model: Human MOS: P.808-lite ACR protocol (3-5 listener panel)
candidates.repo: Protocol per ITU-T P.808 open-source implementation (github.com/microsoft/P.808); optional UI: webMUSHRA or a static randomized HTML page
candidates.license: P.808 toolkit MIT; protocol itself is a standard
languages: Run separately per language with native or fluent listeners for EN, MSA Arabic, Hindi; instructions translated per language
candidates.install: No ML install needed. Recommended lightweight design: (1) 5-point ACR scale (1 bad - 5 excellent), rate NATURALNESS only; (2) each listener hears every item, presentation order independently randomized per listener (a small Python script that shuffles and writes per-listener playlists/HTML is enough); (3) hidden upper anchor = real human recordings of the same texts, hidden lower anchor = deliberately degraded sample (e.g. low-pass filtered or legacy concatenative TTS) mixed invisibly into the list to verify raters use the scale; (4) 1-2 trap/attention items (repeat clip or silence); (5) headphones, quiet room, loudness-normalized clips (e.g. -23 LUFS); (6) per-language instructions stating 'judge how natural/human the voice sounds, not the content'; (7) report per-system mean +/- 95% CI, optionally per-rater z-normalization before averaging
candidates.params_size: n/a
candidates.mac_feasibility: Fully local; audio prep with pydub/ffmpeg on the M2
candidates.quality_evidence: Design borrows from ITU-T P.808 (ACR, 5 votes/stimulus target, data-screening via traps - the open-source implementation approved ~88.7% of submissions with default filters) and MUSHRA's hidden reference+anchor concept. Disclose honestly: 3-5 listeners is far below P.808's crowd scale, so present human MOS as indicative with wide CIs, use it to validate the ranking from predicted MOS rather than as calibrated absolute scores - this caveat itself is a credibility win in a case study

summary: Recommended eval stack for the 8GB M2: predicted MOS via pip-installable Distill-MOS (primary, MIT, CPU-fast) plus torch.hub UTMOS22-strong (for literature comparability, avoiding the broken fairseq install) and NISQA-TTS (best cross-lingual generalization, but CC BY-NC-SA weights); all are English-trained, so for Arabic/Hindi report deltas against real-speech anchors per language and disclose that absolute cross-language MOS comparisons are invalid (IndicMOS showed open predictors degrade on Indian languages). Speaker similarity: SpeechBrain spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb as the single primary metric (pip install speechbrain, 192-dim, CPU-fast) — crucially, cosine thresholds are model-specific: ECAPA same-speaker pairs land ~0.4-0.7 with a 0.25 default decision threshold, while wavlm-base-plus-sv's card uses 0.86 and Resemblyzer clusters ~0.75-0.9, so calibrate with same/different-speaker pairs from your own reference data instead of quoting a universal 0.75. Round-trip WER: faster-whisper large-v3-turbo int8 on CPU (~1.5GB RAM, ~4-5x realtime) as the workhorse for all three languages, whisper.cpp Metal q5_0 as a faster alternative, full large-v3 on the Colab T4 as cross-check; Arabic MSA is fine with Whisper (FLEURS ~4-8% WER) provided you compute normalized WER with camel-tools (dediacritization + alef/hamza/alef-maksura/taa-marbuta unification); Hindi needs an Indic cross-check (IndicConformer-600M or IndicWhisper, since whisper large-v3 Hindi WER is ~19%) and indic-nlp-library normalization — never Whisper's BasicTextNormalizer, which strips Devanagari matras; report CER alongside WER for both. Human MOS: a P.808-lite ACR protocol with per-listener randomization, hidden real-speech upper anchors, a degraded lower anchor, trap items, and native listeners per language, reported as mean with 95% CI and framed as indicative given the 3-5 person panel.
